title: Applications of Depth Estimation
concept: Depth estimation powers autonomous navigation, augmented reality, 3D scene reconstruction, and portrait mode photography — any application needing a computer to understand not just what it sees, but how far away it is.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why does depth estimation evaluation use continuous error metrics instead of exact-match accuracy?

options.text: Because depth is continuous, a numerically close prediction should be rewarded, not just an exact match
Because depth predictions are always either perfectly right or completely wrong
Because continuous metrics cannot be computed for images

exercises.explain: Yes! Continuous depth values need continuous-valued evaluation.

cards.title: Distance touches almost every real-world vision product
cards.body: Once a system understands 'how far', a huge range of applications open up — from a car safely stopping at the right moment, to a phone blurring a photo's background realistically, to a robot navigating a cluttered room.

cards.title: Real-World Depth Applications
cards.body: Depth estimation powers: autonomous navigation (self-driving cars, robots avoiding obstacles), augmented reality (placing virtual objects realistically in a real scene), 3D scene reconstruction (building 3D models from photos), and portrait mode photography (blurring backgrounds based on estimated subject distance).

cards.title: Giving every camera a sense of touch
cards.body: Regular vision is like seeing a room through a window — you know what's there but can't feel how far away it is. Depth estimation is like reaching your hand through that window to physically feel the distance to everything, letting a system 'understand' the scene the way touch would.

cards.title: Smartphone portrait mode
cards.body: When you take a 'portrait mode' photo, your phone estimates depth to figure out which pixels belong to the nearby subject (kept sharp) versus the distant background (blurred), creating that professional camera 'bokeh' effect entirely through depth-aware computation.

cards.title: Not every depth application needs the same precision
cards.body: It's a myth that every depth application needs laser-precise, millimeter-accurate measurements. Portrait mode blur only needs 'roughly this pixel is near, that pixel is far' — a much looser requirement than a self-driving car needing centimeter-precise distance to avoid a collision. Application requirements should drive the choice between monocular, stereo, or LiDAR-based depth.

cards.title: Precision Needs Across Depth Applications
cards.body: Matching depth precision to the task:

left.text:
Portrait mode background blur
General AR object placement
Monocular depth often sufficient

right.text:
Self-driving collision avoidance
Precision robotic manipulation
Requires stereo/LiDAR-grade accuracy

exercises.prompt: What real-world feature does smartphone 'portrait mode' rely on?

options.text: Depth estimation, to separate the nearby subject from the distant background for blurring

exercises.explain: Correct! Portrait mode uses depth-aware computation to create realistic background blur.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Every depth estimation application requires the same millimeter-level precision, regardless of use case.
exercises.explain: False. Precision requirements vary widely — portrait mode blur needs far less precision than self-driving collision avoidance.

exercises.sentence: Application requirements should drive the choice between monocular, stereo, or ___-based depth methods.
exercises.explain: The right depth method depends on how much precision the specific application actually needs.

exercises.prompt: Why can smartphone portrait mode typically rely on monocular depth estimation, while a self-driving car usually cannot rely on monocular depth alone?

options.text: Portrait mode blur tolerates rougher depth precision, while collision avoidance needs much higher accuracy that monocular alone often can't guarantee
Smartphones physically cannot run any depth algorithm
Self-driving cars never use any camera-based sensing

exercises.explain: Correct! Precision requirements differ dramatically based on the real-world stakes of the application.

exercises.prompt: We have completed our study of Depth Estimation — stereo and monocular methods, ground-truth data, and real-world applications. What is the next logical computer vision topic to study?

options.text: 3D Vision — representing and processing full 3D scenes using point clouds and Neural Radiance Fields
Restarting the curriculum from classical image filtering
Deleting all depth estimation models and starting over
Skipping directly to deployment without further vision topics

exercises.explain: Exactly! Now that we can estimate distance per pixel, let's go further into full 3D scene representation. Next up: 3D Vision — Point Clouds and NeRFs!
